content:
I am a software engineer on leave of absence from the College of William and Mary’s M.S./Ph.D. program in computer science in order to pursue industry work.
I graduated summa cum laude from Longwood University in 2019 with a B.S. in computer science and a minor in chemistry. At Longwood, I served as the president of the university’s chapter of UPE and as vice president of ACM.
In my free time, I enjoy (re)creating video games, specifically arcade games.
summary:

title: Jacob Carney

content:
R@ Slayer is a game I have been writing since freshman year of undergrad. Currently it is stuck in development limbo. It is typically one of my go to projects whenever I learn a new programming language or graphics library.
Originally, it was created as a final project for my CMSC 162: Introduction to Algorithmic Design II class at Longwood University. My project partners were Jacob Hudgins and Brendan Speed. The original version was written using curses for the interface. Maze floors were generated by recursive backtracking. A variety of items were used for interacting with the dungeon: an upgradeable lantern to be able to see more of the dungeon, bombs and big bombs to destroy walls, a compass to guide the player to the staircase, and coins in order to afford all these extravagent amenities. The game could be saved and loaded.
A second, graphical, version of the game was implemented using Python with Pygame for the “engine”. Textures and sprites were borrowed from a Minecraft texture pack. Some of the features, such as the lantern, were not included in favor of a more advanced visibility system (which never got implemented). Cheat codes were included in this version of the game (for debugging purposes of course). I had intended to make this project a group effort, so the repo belongs to a group formed at HackNC. This perpetually incomplete version of the game can be found here.
The third iteration of the game is currently in development limbo. This one returns to C++ with Allegro 5 for graphics and a homebrew “engine” on top of it. Health potions have been introduced as well as a leveling system. Menus for stores have been reintroduced and a pause menu has been created (however there is still no text on these screens). I am still using assets from the Minecraft texture pack for the purpose of creation and testing of the game.
A fourth (and hopefully final) implementation of this game with an emphasis on a new method of dungeon generation, which is inspired by the dungeon generation used by TinyKeep, and player visibility.
title: R@ Slayer (Pronounced Rat Slayer)

content:
While I was following the University of Helsinki’s Full Stack Open course, I decided one of the projects I should work on would be a food tracker. I have loosely used Fitbit’s food tracking functionality since I am a Fitbit user. However, I intended the focus of the tracker to be visualizing various metrics / breakdowns of a user’s diet. I settled on using Nutritionix for the nutrition lookup and d3.js for the visualization framework.
The application has basic functionality for logging in, logging an entry based either on entered information or UPC lookup (no barcode scanning), and charting the breakdown of calories by meal and macros.
Frontend
Backend
summary: A simple food tracking application focused on visualization.
title: Food Tracker

content:
One of the things I enjoy doing in my free time (when I get free time) is rewriting arcade games. I have cloned games like Breakout and Tetris. Both of these games are implemented in C++ using Allegro 5 for keyboard input, audio, and graphics.
My current project is a clone of a game called DeathBall, which I discovered at an arcade in Morgantown, West Virginia. I decided to use Unity to recreate this game since it required a bit more physics than I was willing to try implementing on my own. I do not plan to post this one on Github for potential issues with copywrite.
summary: Cloned arcade games for personal use.
title: Homebrew Arcade Games

summary: Parabens, ester derivatives of para-hydroxybenzoic acid, are commonly used in cosmetic items as antimicrobial and antifungal agents. Recent studies have discovered a correlation between paraben  use  and  breast  cancer  due  to  the  xenoestrogenic  properties  of  parabens.  This experiment was designed to synthesize a paraben that does not activate estrogen receptors but is still antimicrobial in nature. By substituting bromine atoms onto the aromatic ring of the paraben, its capability of activating the estrogen receptor is nullified. The antimicrobial properties of the substituted paraben were then confirmed by comparing its ability to kill Streptococcus salivarius to various traditional parabens in a serial dilution assay on a 96-well plate. The substituted butylparaben created had a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 128 micrograms per milliliter (μg/mL) for S. salivarius. Butylparaben, the traditional paraben counterpart, has been determined to have a MIC of 128μg/mL for S. salivarius. Thus, the brominated paraben we synthesized killed S. salivarius as well as its traditional paraben counterpart, confirming that bromination does not inhibit its ability to kill bacteria. This study in conjunction with past studies illustrating substituted parabens are weaker xenoestrogens supports the possibility that they are safer commercial alternatives.
title: Synthesis of 3,5-substituted Parabens and their Antimicrobial Properties

content:
FooBar++ is a project I worked on for my Senior Capstone in Computer Science at Longwood University. It is a web-based system for Computer Science 160 and 162 students to test their code for projects and for instructors to assign work. Students will be able to work in a safe, sandbox environment. This system is built around operation on an internal network. It is intended to be run on Linux- or UNIX-based machines.
The other members of my team were:
 Heather Switzer Ben Byrnes Jonathan Gunter Joseph Harp Brandon Lewis Tyler Zamora-Carden
summary: A web application for an introductory C++ course.
title: FooBar++

content:
During the summer of 2017, Longwood University’s Computer Science department moved from the basement of the Hardy House into unused rooms in Stevens Hall. One of the minor inconveniences that came with the move was that the new lab had energy efficient lights that automatically turned off when no activity was detected. The motion sensors were blocked by a wall, making the front of the room (where there are no computers), the only place visible to the sensors. Students would not be able to just stand up or wave at the sensor to activate it. This meant that sometimes, the lights would not come back on unless someone new entered the lab.
Longwood’s chapter of ACM wanted to create a silly way for students to turn the lights back on without needing to leave their work.
The solution was silly: “What if we had a way to tweet at the lab and turn on the lights with a servo powered arm?”
We set up a Twitter account for the lab and we created a Twitter bot in Python to listen for @ mentions to the lab account. The bot used the googletrans module to tweet a quote back at whoever requested light in a random language. The code for both the light bot and the servo are available on Github.
This project has since been deprecated in favor of a newer project headed by Cameron Haddock that I assume is more practical.
summary: A Twitter bot that listens for mentions in order to turn on lights in the computer lab.
title: Twitter Light Bot
